---
title: "Combinatorial surface-marker targets for prostate cancer"
subtitle: "Patient-robust discovery of AND / NOT dual-antigen pairs, benchmarked against PSMA-PSCA"
author: "William Guesdon &nbsp;·&nbsp; Built with Claude: Life Sciences &nbsp;·&nbsp; Research Track 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme_dark.scss]
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    slide-number: false
    embed-resources: true
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

## The project

**Antigen-directed therapies struggle to hit the tumor without hitting healthy tissue.** Few surface
antigens are truly absent from every normal cell type, so single-target CAR-T, T-cell engagers and ADCs
carry on-target off-tumor toxicity.

[Logic-gated targeting]{.accent} engages a cell only when two conditions hold:

- an **AND gate** &nbsp;— both markers present on the tumor
- a **NOT gate** &nbsp;— an activator on, unless a blocker marks a healthy cell to spare

::: {.muted}
**The question:** which pair of cell-surface proteins marks prostate cancer cells while sparing every
healthy human cell type? We score marker pairs **per patient** in single-cell data, benchmarked against
the known **PSMA-PSCA** pair.
:::

::: {.notes}
About a minute. Most targeted therapies for solid tumors fail on safety, not potency: a single antigen is
almost never absent from every healthy tissue. Logic-gated targeting fixes this by requiring two
conditions before a cell is engaged. We ask, for prostate cancer, which pair of surface proteins marks
the tumor while sparing every healthy cell type, scoring every pair per patient and benchmarking against
PSMA-PSCA, a pair we already know works.
:::

## Built with Claude

::: {.incremental}
- **Flexible and composable** — Claude Code drives our whole stack from one prompt: **Podman R / Seurat /
  Bioconductor containers** and the **AWS CLI**
- **We made it our own** — a custom **CLAUDE.md** (our rules and guardrails) and a **SESSION_SUMMARY**
  working memory keep it consistent and context-aware across sessions
- **Drove the analysis end to end** — datasets, the per-patient scan, doublet removal, the HuPSA
  replication, uncertainty and label-leakage controls, verified citations
- **Reviewed itself** — a **multi-agent adversarial peer review** of the report, then applied the fixes
- **Claude Science** — its **MCP integrations** to external data, used to **validate our findings** and
  mine the literature (AND / NOT methods, the dual-target landscape, datasets, report review)
:::

::: {.notes}
About a minute, the Claude Use story, and the theme is flexibility. Claude Code drives our whole stack
from one prompt: Podman containers running R, Seurat and Bioconductor, and our AWS CLI. We made it our
own with a custom CLAUDE.md holding our rules and guardrails and a SESSION_SUMMARY working memory, so it
stays consistent and context-aware across sessions. It drove the analysis end to end, found and vetted
the datasets, ran the per-patient scan, doublet removal, the HuPSA replication, the uncertainty and
leakage controls, and verified every citation. It even reviewed its own report with a multi-agent
adversarial workflow and applied the fixes. And Claude Science, through its MCP integrations to external
data, is where we validate our findings and mine the literature.
:::

## The analysis and the results

::: {.incremental}
- **Sanity check passed** — the scan re-identifies the known **PSMA-PSCA** pair and its **~6x** safety
  collapse (0.9 &rarr; 0.15), so the method is accurate, not just plausible
- **A new candidate** — nominates **PSMA x STEAP1**, Pareto-optimal among the clean pairs; both antigens
  already have clinical-grade binders
- **No usable NOT gate** — no activator/blocker pair cleared the liability bar (a negative result)
- **It caught its own limit** — an independent **369k-cell** cohort confirms tumor-specificity but shows
  the pair is lost in metastatic and neuroendocrine disease
- **Delivered** — a reproducible **Quarto report**, a **website**, and a **live app**
:::

::: {.notes}
About a minute, the results. First, the trust signal: the scan re-identifies the known PSMA-PSCA pair and
its roughly sixfold safety collapse, so the method re-finds a known answer, proof it is accurate. Second,
it nominates a new candidate, PSMA times STEAP1, Pareto-optimal among the clean pairs, with clinical
binders already in humans. Third, the NOT gate found nothing usable, a negative result. Fourth, an
independent 369-thousand-cell cohort confirms tumor
specificity but catches a real limitation, the pair is lost in advanced disease. And we ship a
reproducible Quarto report, a website, and a live app.
:::
